revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.comment: Programmatically generating so that if this gets imported to wikipedia as a draft, the bot will attribute it to Immanuelle and send them g6 warnings

revision.comment: とりあえず・・
revision.text:
[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おみた、おんた)は神社等の神事に使われる水田であり、「神田」(かんだ)とも呼ばれる。この田の有る地域には地名として「神田」を付けている地方多い。 '''御田'''が存在する神社などは、主に稲、農業の神様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の御田があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、皇居内にも'''御田'''が有り、天皇陛下自ら田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 行事としての'''御田'''は田植えの時期などが現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の水田の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 現在の早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな祭りになる事がある。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[神田]] *[[皇居]] *[[伊勢神宮]] **[[伊雑宮]] - 伊勢神宮の御田のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） *[[日本三大一覧]] {{stub}}

revision.text:
[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]

'''御神田'''(おみた、おんた)は[[神社]]等の神事に使われる水田であり、「神田」(かんだ)とも呼ばれる。この田の有る地域には地名として「神田」を付けている地方が多い。 '''御神田'''が存在する神社などは、主に[[稲]]、農業の神様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の御神田があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、皇居内にも'''御田'''が有り、[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 行事としての'''御神田'''は田植えの時期などが現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の水田の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 現在の早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな祭りになる事がある。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[神田]] *[[皇居]] *[[伊勢神宮]] **[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] **[[伊雑宮]]　-　伊勢神宮の御田のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） **[[神田明神]]（東京都）　－　元伊勢神宮の御田があった事から名付けられた神社 {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御神田'''(おみた、おんた)は[[神社]]等の神事に使われる水田であり、「神田」(かんだ)とも呼ばれる。この田の有る地域には地名として「神田」を付けている地方が多い。 '''御神田'''が存在する神社などは、主に[[稲]]、農業の神様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の御神田があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、皇居内にも'''御田'''が有り、[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 ==行事== 行事としての'''御神田'''は田植えの時期が現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな祭りになる事がある。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[神田]] *[[皇居]] *[[伊勢神宮]] **[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] **[[伊雑宮]]　-　伊勢神宮の御田のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） **[[神田明神]]（東京都）　－　元伊勢神宮の御田があった事から名付けられた神社 {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:Izawa-mitaue 02.JPG|thumb|256px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御神田'''(おみた、おんた)は[[神社]]等の神事に使われる水田であり、「神田」(かんだ)とも呼ばれる。この田の有る地域には地名として「神田」を付けている地方が多い。 '''御神田'''が存在する神社などは、主に[[稲]]、農業の神様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の御神田があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、皇居内にも'''御田'''が有り、[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 ==行事== 行事としての'''御神田'''は田植えの時期が現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな祭りになる事がある。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[神田]] *[[皇居]] *[[伊勢神宮]] **[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] **[[伊雑宮]]　-　伊勢神宮の御田のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） **[[神田明神]]（東京都）　－　元伊勢神宮の御田があった事から名付けられた神社 {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)は[[神社]]等の[[神事]]に使われる[[水田]]であり、その水田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「御神田」「[[神田]]」(かんだ)とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は水田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 '''御田'''が存在する神社などは、主に[[稲]]、[[農業]]の[[神]]様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の'''御田'''があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、[[皇居]]内にも'''御田'''が有り、[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 ==行事== 行事としての'''御田'''、'''御神田'''は田植えの時期が現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな[[祭り]]になる事がある。 [[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[田植]] *伊勢神宮 **[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] **[[伊雑宮]]　-　伊勢神宮の'''御田'''のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|三重県伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)は[[神社]]等の[[神事]]に使われる[[水田]]であり、その水田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「御神田」「[[神田]]」(かんだ)とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は水田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 '''御田'''が存在する神社などは、主に[[稲]]、[[農業]]の[[神]]様を祀っているところが多い。[[伊勢神宮]]などでは、2ヶ所の'''御田'''があり、大々的に神事が行われている。また、[[皇居]]内にも'''御田'''が有り、[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行っている。 ==行事== 行事としての'''御田'''、'''御神田'''は田植えの時期が現在よりも１月程度遅く行われる。これは、昔の歳時記に乗っ取って行われる為、早場米の多くなった現在の田植え、稲刈りの時期とは異なる事が多い。 早場米に比べ、時期が遅いため稲穂に実が入りやすく、同じ種の米でも美味しい米が多いとも言われている。 田植え時には、大きな[[祭り]]になる事がある。 [[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 また、地方によっては[[赤米]](あかまい)と呼ばれている種類の米を作る場合もある。これは赤飯の原点ではとも言われている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[田植]] *伊勢神宮 **[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] **[[伊雑宮]]　-　伊勢神宮の'''御田'''のある宮（日本三大御田植え祭りが行われる） {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

revision.comment: 不適切な記述の除去を中心に編集。
revision.text:
[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|内宮別宮伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)は[[神社]]が所有する[[水田]]であり、その水田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「御神田」「[[神田]]」(かんだ)とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は水田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 [[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で神社の領地（神領）が廃止されたため、一時的に日本全国の神社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された神社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行う。 ==行事== 田植え時に[[祭り]]を伴うことがあり、20世紀以降に伝承された祭りのうち、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 == 関連項目 == *[[稲作]] *[[田植]] *[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|内宮別宮伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)は[[神社]]等が所有する[[水田]]であり、その水田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「御神田」「[[神田]]」(かんだ)とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は水田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 [[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で神社の領地（神領）が廃止されたため、一時的に日本全国の神社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された神社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが田植えと稲刈りを行う。 ==行事== 日本人と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に伝承されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 == 関連項目 == *[[田植]] *[[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

[[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|none|内宮別宮伊雑宮の御田植祭]]
'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)とは、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する領[[田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]](かんだ)」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 [[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（寺社領）が廃止されたため、一時的に日本全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植]]と稲刈りを行う。 ==行事== 日本人と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に伝承されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 == 関連項目 == *[[朱印地]] *[[御田植]] *[[御田舞]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]]

'''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)とは、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する領[[田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]](かんだ)」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。 [[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（寺社領）が廃止されたため、一時的に日本全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植]]と稲刈りを行う。 ==行事== 日本人と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に伝承されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。 == 関連項目 == *[[朱印地]] *[[御田植]] *[[御田舞]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:神道|おみた]] [[Category:米|おみた]] [[Category:予祝芸能|おみた]]

{{otheruses|寺社や皇室等が所有する領田|食べ物のおでん|おでん}} [[Image:izawanomiya_2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|内宮別宮伊雑宮の御田植祭]] '''御田'''(おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん)とは、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する領[[田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]](かんだ)」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。
[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（寺社領）が廃止されたため、一時的に日本全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植]]と稲刈りを行う。
==行事== 日本人と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に伝承されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 関連項目 == *[[朱印地]] *[[御田植]] *[[御田舞]]

==行事== 日本人と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に伝承されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 関連項目 == *[[朱印地]] *[[御田植]] *[[御田舞]] *[[田の神]]

revision.comment: /* 行事 */ 御田祭は、熊野本宮大社だけのものではありません。いくらでもあります。
revision.text:

== 関連項目 == *[[朱印地]] *[[御田植]] *[[御田舞]] *[[田の神]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses|寺社や皇室等が所有する領田|食べ物のおでん|おでん}} '''御田'''（おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん）は、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する領[[田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[行事]]のことも指す。「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]]（かんだ）」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し、行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ、'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。
[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植]]と[[稲刈り]]を行う。
== 行事 == [[日本人]]と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に[[伝承]]されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
=== その他の御田祭 === その他、以下を含め多くの御田祭が伝承されている（但し全てが重要無形民俗文化財に指定されているわけではない）。 *[[熊野本宮大社例大祭#御田祭（4月15日）|熊野本宮大社の御田祭]] - [[熊野本宮大社]]（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]本宮町）の[[熊野本宮大社例大祭|例大祭]]の一部をなす祭礼 *[http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/K/es-12.htm 丹生都比売神社の御田祭] - [[和歌山県]][[かつらぎ町]]の[[丹生都比売神社]]の神事 *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/db/result.php?id=4080 猿田彦神社の御田祭] - [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]の[[猿田彦神社]]の神事 *[http://www.1300.jp/event/detail/roam/0350004.html 鏡作神社の御田祭] - [[奈良県]][[田原本町]]の鏡作神社の神事 *[http://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~kyonosato/kyotofu/fu05/fu05onda08yasaka.html 尾長野八坂神社の御田祭] - [[京都府]][[京丹波町]]の尾長野八坂神社の神事 *[http://www7b.biglobe.ne.jp/~kyonosato/gyoji/07/07onda07.html 松尾大社の御田祭] - [[京都市]][[西京区]]の[[松尾大社]]の神事 *[http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/event/spring.html 住吉神社の御田祭] - [[福岡市]][[博多区]]の[[住吉神社 (福岡市)|住吉神社]]の神事 *[http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/313.htm 田代神社の御田祭] - [[宮崎県]][[美郷町 (宮崎県)|美郷町]]（旧[[西郷村 (宮崎県)|西郷村]]）の田代神社の神事
御田祭の他、[[御田植]]・[[御田舞]]・[[田植踊]]・[[田遊び]]等、多くの稲作に関わる行事や芸能が伝承されている。
== 関連項目 == *[[神郡]] *[[朱印地・黒印地]] *[[田の神]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おみた}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:稲作]] [[Category:米]] [[Category:神道]] [[Category:予祝芸能]]

[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植]]と[[稲刈り]]を行う。
== 行事 == [[日本人]]と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に[[伝承]]されており、[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は、[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。

御田祭の他、[[御田植]]・[[御田舞]]・[[田植踊]]・[[田遊び]]等、多くの稲作に関わる行事や芸能が伝承されている。
== 関連項目 == *[[神郡]] *[[朱印地・黒印地]] *[[田の神]]

[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。

revision.comment: /* 外部リンク */
revision.text:
{{otheruses|寺社や皇室等が所有する領田|食べ物のおでん|おでん}} '''御田'''（おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん）は、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する[[田|領田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[行事]]<ref>「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]]（かんだ）」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し、行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ、'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。</ref>。
[[日本人]]と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に[[伝承]]されている。[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定され、[[御田植祭]]、[[田遊び]]、[[田植踊]]と呼ばれる行事や芸能なども伝承されている。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植え]]と[[稲刈り]]を行う。
[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[神郡]] * [[朱印地・黒印地]] * [[田の神]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/top_guide/menu_kanko/seasons/ondasai.html 丹生都比売神社の御田祭] - [[和歌山県]][[かつらぎ町]]の[[丹生都比売神社]]の神事 *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/event/detail_35088.html 猿田彦神社の御田祭] - [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]の[[猿田彦神社]]の神事 *[http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/event/spring.html 住吉神社の御田祭] - [[福岡市]][[博多区]]の[[住吉神社 (福岡市)|住吉神社]]の神事 *[http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm 田代神社の御田祭] - [[宮崎県]][[美郷町 (宮崎県)|美郷町]]の田代神社の神事
{{DEFAULTSORT:おみた}} [[Category:稲作]] [[Category:神道]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。

revision.comment: Created page with "{{otheruses|Rice paddies owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine padd..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Rice paddies owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Tanokami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano no Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, Wakayama Prefecture * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Sarutahiko Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Sumiyoshi Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社}} in {{ill|Hakata-ku|ja|博多区}}, Fukuoka City * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Tashiro Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in {{ill|Misato, Miyazaki|ja|美郷町}}, Miyazaki Prefecture
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Rice cultivation]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Fertility rituals]]
[[ja:御田]]

In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.

Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.

Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

[[ja:御田]]

revision.comment: adding new pages tag because that is one I use for finding new links
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Rice paddies owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Tanokami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano no Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, Wakayama Prefecture * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Sarutahiko Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Sumiyoshi Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社}} in {{ill|Hakata-ku|ja|博多区}}, Fukuoka City * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Tashiro Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in {{ill|Misato, Miyazaki|ja|美郷町}}, Miyazaki Prefecture

revision.comment: Created page with "{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifical..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Notes == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Rice farming]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Ritual performing arts]] [[ja:御田]]

However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.

The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Notes == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Rice paddies owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Tanokami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano no Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, Wakayama Prefecture * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Sarutahiko Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Sumiyoshi Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社}} in {{ill|Hakata-ku|ja|博多区}}, Fukuoka City * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Tashiro Shrine's Ota Festival] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in {{ill|Misato, Miyazaki|ja|美郷町}}, Miyazaki Prefecture
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.

== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.

In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.

== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>

Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.

At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.

[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:御田]]

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
