revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references />== See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=draft}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊|12=draft}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|12=draft}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} maintains two Ota fields today.

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=draft}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|12=draft}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|12=draft}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=draft}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|12=draft}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居|12=draft}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|12=draft}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室|12=draft}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=draft}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|12=draft}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|12=draft}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人|12=draft}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=draft}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊|12=draft}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|12=draft}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社|12=draft}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社|12=draft}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)|12=draft}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

/* 外部リンク */
revision.text:
{{otheruses|寺社や皇室等が所有する領田|その他|おでん (曖昧さ回避)}} '''御田'''（おた、みた、おみた、おんた、おんだ、おでん）は、[[寺社]]や[[皇室]]等が所有する[[田|領田]]のこと、またその領田で行われる[[イベント|行事]]<ref>「[[寺田]]（てらだ）」、「[[神田]]（かんだ）」「御神田」とも呼ばれるが、一般に「'''寺田'''」「'''神田'''」と呼ぶ場合は領田そのもののことを指し、行事のことは指さない。また[[田楽]]と同義にも使われ、'''おでん'''と読んだ場合は一般に[[料理]]の[[おでん]]のことを指す。</ref>。
[[日本人]]と[[稲作]]の深い関わりを示すものとして、多くの御田に関する行事が日本各地に[[伝承]]されている。[[田原の御田]]、[[磯部の御神田]]、[[吉良川の御田祭]]は[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定され、[[御田植祭]]、[[田遊び]]、[[田植踊]]と呼ばれる行事や芸能なども伝承されている。[[皇居]]内の御田では[[天皇]]自らが[[田植え]]と[[稲刈り]]を行う。
[[明治]]初頭の[[上知令]]で寺社の領地（[[寺社領]]）が廃止されたため、一時的に[[日本]]全国の寺社の御田が消滅した。その後周辺の住民の寄贈などにより御田が再興された寺社があり、[[伊勢神宮]]は2ヶ所に御田を残している。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[神郡]] * [[朱印地・黒印地]] * [[田の神]]
== 外部リンク == *[https://niutsuhime.or.jp/information/ondamatsuri/ 天野の御田祭] - [[和歌山県]][[かつらぎ町]]の[[丹生都比売神社]]の神事 *[https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/minzoku/1002213.html 猿田彦神社の御田祭] - [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]の民俗文化財 *[https://www.nihondaiichisumiyoshigu.jp/rituals/ 祭典･行事] - [[福岡市]][[博多区]]の[[住吉神社 (福岡市)|住吉神社]]の神事 *[http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm 田代神社の御田祭] - [[宮崎県]][[美郷町 (宮崎県)|美郷町]]の田代神社の神事
{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おみた}} [[Category:稲作]] [[Category:神道]] [[Category:予祝芸能]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dengaku|qq=}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oden|qq=}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace (Japan)|qq=}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household|qq=}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dengaku|qq=}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oden|qq=}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese people|qq=}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terasharyo|qq=}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-no-Kami|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niutsuhime Shrine|qq=}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|qq=}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Otaue Matsuri]] → [[Otaue Festival]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dengaku|qq=}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oden|qq=}}.</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the {{ill|Imperial Palace (Japan)|ja|皇居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace (Japan)|qq=}}, the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}}, {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}, or the {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|皇室|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household|qq=}}, as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dengaku|qq=}}, and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish {{ill|Oden|ja|おでん|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oden|qq=}}.</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between {{ill|Japanese people|ja|日本人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese people|qq=}} and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|皇居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}} personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ({{ill|terasharyo|ja|寺社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terasharyo|qq=}}), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-no-Kami|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the {{ill|Niutsuhime Shrine|ja|丹生都比売神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niutsuhime Shrine|qq=}} in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Shrine|qq=}} in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|qq=}} in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.

{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[Ise City]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

<!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--> [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

<!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--> [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]].

<!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--> [[Category:予祝芸能]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sacred rice field]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[Meiji period]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].
