revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[shrines]], [[temples]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

<!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--><!--Sacred rice field--> [[Category:Pre-celebration Performing Arts]] [[sn:Sacred rice field]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|qq=|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|qq=|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|qq=|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|qq=|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.

Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|qq=|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|qq=|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|qq=|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|qq=|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:御田]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Omita|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|1=Isobe no Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Isobe no Omita" to "Isobe-no-Omita"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:御田]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe-no-Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe-no-Omita|1=Isobe-no-Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.
== Overview == '''Ota''' (御田; おた, みた, おみた, おんた, おんだ, おでん) refers to rice paddies owned by [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]], [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]], or the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Household]], as well as events held in connection with these paddies<ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|1=Isobe-no-Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].
{{Shinto-stub}}

In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>.
As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan. Events such as the {{ill|Otamatsuri|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otamatsuri|1=Otamatsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe no Goshinden|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe no Goshinden|1=Isobe-no-Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Otamatsuri|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Otamatsuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. Other customs and performances, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue-sai|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue-sai|qq=}}, {{ill|Taasobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taasobi|1=ta-asobi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577415}}, and {{ill|Rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taue Odori|qq=}}, have also been preserved. At the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally plants and harvests rice in the Ota fields.
In the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the {{ill|Land Reclamation Ordinance|ja|上知令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Reclamation Ordinance|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q702796}} abolished temple and shrine-owned land ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|terasharyo]]), temporarily eliminating Ota fields across Japan. Some temples and shrines later revived their Ota fields through donations from local residents. [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] maintains two Ota fields today.
== Notes == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|ja|朱印地・黒印地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuinchi and Kokuinchi|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11521520}} * [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Ta-no-Kami]]
== External Links == * [http://www.town.katsuragi.wakayama.jp/070/040/20170330113330.html Amano Ota Festival] - A ritual at the [[:en:Niutsuhime Shrine|Niutsuhime Shrine]] in [[Katsuragi Town]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]]. * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/9198.htm Ota Festival of Sarutahiko Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sarutahiko Shrine|Sarutahiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. * [http://chikuzen-sumiyoshi.or.jp/sp/event/spring.html#01 Ota Festival of Sumiyoshi Shrine] - A ritual at the [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)]] in [[Hakata Ward]], [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]]. * [http://www.town.miyazaki-misato.lg.jp/2535.htm Ota Festival of Tashiro Shrine] - A ritual at Tashiro Shrine in [[Misato Town]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:御田]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11489223]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|Territorial rice fields owned by temples, shrines, or the imperial family|other uses|Oden (disambiguation)}} '''Ota''' (御田) refers to [[:en:paddy_field|territorial rice fields]] owned by [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]], [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]], or the [[:en:Imperial family|Imperial family]], as well as the [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] or [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] conducted in these fields.<ref>The fields are also called "寺田" (Terada) or "神田" (Kanda), but these terms typically refer to the land itself, while the term "御田" refers specifically to the associated rituals. Additionally, the term can overlap with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]] practices. When read as "Oden," it generally refers to the [[:en:Japanese cuisine|Japanese cuisine]] of the same name.</ref><ref>Also called "{{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}" (temple paddies), "{{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=shinden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855080}}" (divine paddies), or "Goshinden." In general, "Terada" or "Kanda" refers specifically to the paddies, while "御田" often refers to events. The term is sometimes used synonymously with [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], and when read as "Oden," it typically refers to the dish [[:en:Oden|Oden]].</ref>
As an expression of the close relationship between the [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and [[rice cultivation]], many rituals associated with Ota have been passed down across [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Events such as the {{ill|Tahara no Ota|ja|田原の御田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahara no Ota|1=Tahara no Ota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576232}}, {{ill|Isobe-no-Omita|ja|磯部の御神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isobe-no-Omita|1=Isobe-no-Omita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588063}}, and {{ill|Kira River Ota Festival|ja|吉良川の御田祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kira River Ota Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御田八幡宮|1=Onda-hachimangu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489224}} have been designated as [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]]. Additional practices, such as {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Ta-asobi|ja|田遊び|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-asobi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Rice Planting Dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Planting Dance|1=taue odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577057}}, are also preserved as significant cultural heritage. In the [[:en:Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace (Japan)]], the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]] personally participates in [[rice planting]] and [[:en:harvesting|harvesting]] rituals in the dedicated imperial Ota.
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the [[Jōchi-rei]] (Edict of Confiscation of Temple and Shrine Lands) led to the abolition of temple and shrine territories ([[:en:Jisha-ryō|Jisha-ryō]]), temporarily resulting in the disappearance of Ota fields across Japan. However, some shrines and temples restored their Ota fields through donations from local residents. For example, the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] continues to maintain two Ota fields.

As a symbol of the deep connection between [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese people]] and rice cultivation, many traditions surrounding Ota events have been passed down across Japan.
